articles.author: Gisela Salomon, Jack Brook, Sarah Raza
articles.description: In a break from tradition, refugees admitted to the United States after extensive interviews and vetting are being detained and questioned again. The US Department of Homeland Security said in November that the Biden administration's evaluation of roughly 200,000 refugees was inadequate. DHS says it is reviewing cases, starting with about 5,600 refugees who settled in Minnesota. Some have been handcuffed, shackled and flown to Texas for questioning. After being released, they have had to find their own way home. One was asked to sign documents saying that she would voluntarily leave the US. She refused as part of a larger legal fight.
articles.title: Trump administration orders new scrutiny of admitted refugees

articles.author: Michael Hill, Jake Offenhartz
articles.description: Police in New York state are investigating how a nearly blind refugee from Myanmar died after U.S. Border Patrol agents dropped him off at a doughnut shop in Buffalo. Nurul Amin Shah Alam was detained Feb. 19 by agents and let go the same day after authorities determined he wasn't eligible for deportation. He was found dead Tuesday night, five days after being released. Police say the medical examiner ruled out exposure and homicide and called the death health-related. Buffalo Mayor Sean Ryan accused border agents of dereliction of duty. Federal officials say agents left him at a warm, safe location.
articles.title: Police investigate the death of nearly blind refugee in New York state

articles.author: Steve Karnowski
articles.description: The chief federal judge for Minnesota has issued a stern warning to the chief federal prosecutor for the state, as well as to Immigration and Customs Enforcement officials, warning them that they must comply with court orders or they risk criminal contempt charges. Chief Judge Patrick Schiltz is seen as a conservative. But he took issue with an email he received from U.S. Attorney Daniel Rosen, accusing him of overstating the extent of ICE’s noncompliance with court orders arising from the immigration crackdown. His order was just the latest in a series of critical and sometimes scathing statements and rulings by federal judges across the country.
articles.title: Minnesota's chief federal judge issues stern warning on court order compliance

articles.author: Jake Offenhartz
articles.description: Federal immigration agents have detained a Columbia University student in her campus apartment. But hours after she was taken into custody on Thursday, the student was released, after New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani expressed concerns about the arrest to President Donald Trump. The agreement followed an unrelated meeting between the Democratic mayor and Republican president. Attorneys for the student said ICE agents had entered her campus apartment under the guise of searching for a missing person. A spokesperson for the Department of Homeland Security said the woman's visa was terminated in 2016 for failing to attend class. Her attorneys didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
articles.title: Trump agreed to release Columbia student detained by ICE, Mamdani says

articles.author: Stephen Groves
articles.description: Former Secretary of State Hillary Clinton has told members of Congress she had no knowledge of Jeffrey Epstein’s crimes and didn't recall ever meeting him. Thursday's testimony comes at the start of two days of depositions that will include former President Bill Clinton. The depositions are in the Clintons’ hometown of Chappaqua, north of New York City. The depositions come after months of tense back-and-forth with the Republican-controlled House Oversight Committee. Hillary Clinton has said the Democratic couple's knowledge of Epstein is “very limited.” Bill Clinton has not been accused of wrongdoing in his relationship with Epstein. Republicans pledge lengthy days of questioning for both Clintons.
articles.title: Hillary Clinton testifies in House Epstein investigation

articles.author: Sam McNeil
articles.description: The European Commission has asked its anti-fraud watchdog to examine Peter Mandelson’s links to Jeffrey Epstein during his time as the EU’s trade chief. A commission spokesperson confirmed the probe on Thursday after many documents become public. The OLAF watchdog agency has a track record of probing fraud and misconduct across EU institutions, but it does not confirm an investigation. Mandelson faces scrutiny in Britain over alleged misconduct in public office tied to Epstein. His past relationship with Epstein also creates political trouble for Prime Minister Keir Starmer.
articles.title: EU orders probe of Mandelson's Epstein ties while Brussels' trade rep

articles.author: Brian Melley
articles.description: Influential British politician Peter Mandelson is under investigation for alleged misconduct in public office linked to his relationship with the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Those ties forced Mandelson to resign from the ruling Labour Party, step down from the House of Lords, and have threatened the leadership of Prime Minister Keir Starmer. The arrest of Mandelson four days after the former Prince Andrew was taken into custody on similar suspicions are some of the most dramatic consequences of the release of U.S. documents in the Epstein scandal.
articles.title: What to know about UK politician Peter Mandelson's ties to disgraced financier Jeffrey Epstein

articles.description: World Economic Forum head Børge Brende says that he is stepping down after he faced pressure over contacts with the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. Brende, a former Norwegian foreign minister, said in a statement Thursday he had decided “after careful consideration” to step down as president and CEO of the forum, known for its annual January meeting in the Swiss Alpine resort of Davos. He said in the statement released by the WEF that “now is the right moment for the Forum to continue its important work without distractions.”
articles.title: WEF chief Børge Brende quits over Epstein links

articles.author: Linley Sanders
articles.description: A new Gallup poll shows that American sympathies in the Middle East have shifted dramatically toward the Palestinians after decades of overwhelming support for the Israelis. That shift accelerated during the war in Gaza. Fifty-four percent of Americans sympathized more with the Israelis three years ago compared to 31% for the Palestinians. Their support is now about evenly balanced between the Israelis and the Palestinians. The numbers reflect how support for Israel has become deeply contentious in the U.S. with profound implications for American politics and foreign policy. The changing sentiment has been largely driven by Democrats who are now much more likely to sympathize with Palestinians.
articles.title: American sympathy shifts toward Palestinians and away from Israelis: Gallup poll

articles.author: Munir Ahmed, Abdul Qahar Afghan
articles.description: Pakistan defense minister says the country considers itself in an “open war” with neighboring Afghanistan. Defense Minister Khawaja Mohammad Asif made the comments on Friday after Afghanistan launched a cross-border retaliatory attack on Pakistan overnight that saw Islamabad hit back with airstrikes on Kabul in the worst escalation of violence since a Qatar-mediated ceasefire in October. Asif said on X that Pakistan had hoped for peace in Afghanistan after the withdrawal of NATO forces in 2021 and expected the Taliban who seized power in the country to focus on the welfare of the Afghan people and regional stability. Afghanistan said that its military launched its attack late Thursday into Pakistan.
articles.title: Pakistan’s defense minister says that there is an 'open war' with Afghanistan
